# Welcome {.unnumbered}

`sconjoint` is an R package implementing the *structural deep learning*
estimator for forced-choice conjoint experiments developed by @acharya2026structural. The estimator embeds a deep neural network
inside a random-utility logit so that each respondent's preference
vector $\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}(\mathbf Z_i) \in \mathbb{R}^p$ varies
smoothly and flexibly with her observed covariates $\mathbf Z$. Double /
debiased machine learning [DML; @chernozhukov2018double] inference delivers honest
respondent-clustered standard errors on all population-level quantities.

The key advance over the standard AMCE framework
[@hainmueller2014causal] is that $\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}(\mathbf Z_i)$ gives the *joint*
distribution of preferences across attributes for each respondent,
enabling structural quantities --- marginal rates of substitution,
counterfactual choice probabilities, preference polarization,
willingness to pay --- that require the full preference vector rather
than one-attribute-at-a-time marginal effects.

This book is the primary user documentation for the package. It walks
from installation through two complete worked examples, a simulation
sanity check, visualization options, and frequently asked questions.

```{r}
#| label: welcome-load
#| message: false
library(sconjoint)
data(package = "sconjoint")$results[, c("Item", "Title")]
```

## Bundled datasets

The package ships three conjoint datasets from published replication
materials, covering candidate choice, democratic norms, and climate
treaties.

### `sw2022` --- @saha2022welfare: candidate choice

1,191 respondents $\times$ 3 tasks $\times$ 2 profiles = 7,146 rows.

| Column | Role | Levels |
|--------|------|--------|
| `agenda` | Attribute | 3 levels |
| `talent` | Attribute | 3 levels |
| `children` | Attribute | 2 levels |
| `cand_gender` | Attribute | 2 levels |
| `prior_office` | Attribute | 3 levels |
| `resp_female` | Moderator ($\mathbf Z$) | binary |
| `age` | Moderator ($\mathbf Z$) | age in years |
| `pid` | Moderator ($\mathbf Z$) | factor: Democrat, Republican (GOP), Independent |

### `gs2020` --- @graham2020democracy: democratic norms

999 respondents $\times$ $\sim$10 matchups $\times$ 2 profiles = 20,440 rows.

| Column | Role | Levels |
|--------|------|--------|
| `copartisan` | Attribute | Not, Co-partisan |
| `dem_code` | Attribute | 16-level factor (good governance, undemocratic, valence) |
| `cand_sex` | Attribute | Male, Female |
| `cand_race` | Attribute | White, Black, Hispanic, Asian |
| `policy_prox` | Attribute | numeric 0--1 (policy proximity) |
| `resp_ideo` | Moderator ($\mathbf Z$) | 1--7 ideology |
| `resp_pid` | Moderator ($\mathbf Z$) | party ID (7-point) |
| `resp_age` | Moderator ($\mathbf Z$) | age in years |

### `bs2013` --- @bechtel_scheve_2013: climate treaties

2,500 respondents $\times$ 4 tasks $\times$ 2 profiles = 20,000 rows.

| Column | Role | Levels |
|--------|------|--------|
| `distribution` | Attribute | categorical |
| `emissions` | Attribute | categorical |
| `monitoring` | Attribute | categorical |
| `participation` | Attribute | categorical |
| `sanctions` | Attribute | categorical |
| `cost_usd` | Attribute | numeric (USD/month) |
| `resp_female` | Moderator ($\mathbf Z$) | binary |
| `resp_age` | Moderator ($\mathbf Z$) | age in years |
| `resp_ideo` | Moderator ($\mathbf Z$) | 0--10 ideology |

## Organization

The user guide is structured into the following chapters:

-   [Chapter @sec-start]\
    Installation instructions for `torch` and `sconjoint`.

-   [Chapter @sec-sanity]\
    Full workflow on simulated data with known ground truth. Walks through fitting, recovery, structural quantities, and visualization --- verifying each step against the true DGP.

-   [Chapter @sec-sw]\
    Full pipeline on the @saha2022welfare candidate-choice conjoint: data preparation, model fitting, interpretation, all structural quantities, and visualization.

-   [Chapter @sec-gs]\
    Second worked example on the @graham2020democracy democratic-norms conjoint, emphasizing heterogeneity testing, partisan subgroup analysis, and preference clustering.

-   [Chapter @sec-bs]\
    Third worked example on the @bechtel_scheve_2013 climate-treaty conjoint, showcasing willingness-to-pay and compensating differentials.

-   [Chapter @sec-plots]\
    Customizing ridgeline density plots and training loss traces with `ggplot2`.
